walkthrough.title: Gather All the Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Skip-Bo set up! Grab the deck of 162 cards. You will see numbered cards from one through twelve, and some special cards that say Skip-Bo on them. Those Skip-Bo cards are wild cards, and there are 18 of them. Combine everything into one big pile.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Deck
walkthrough.text: Now let's give that big deck a thorough shuffle. With 162 cards, it takes a bit of effort. You can split the deck in half and riffle shuffle a few times, or pile shuffle if you prefer. The goal is to get those Skip-Bo wilds and all the numbers nicely randomized.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Stock Piles
walkthrough.text: Now deal a personal stock pile to each player. For a standard game, deal 30 cards face down to each player. If you want a shorter game, deal 20 cards instead. Each player stacks their cards neatly in a face-down pile in front of them. Do not look at any of the cards.

walkthrough.title: Flip Your Top Card
walkthrough.text: Everyone flip the top card of your stock pile face up. Leave it right on top of the pile so everyone can see it. This is the first card you will try to play during the game. The rest of the pile stays face down underneath.

walkthrough.title: Place the Draw Pile
walkthrough.text: Make sure everyone can reach it. Leave plenty of space next to the draw pile for up to four building piles. These building piles will form during the game as players start playing cards. You are all set!

walkthrough.title: Pick Who Goes First
walkthrough.text: Choose someone to go first however you like. Youngest player, random pick, or whoever won last time. Play will go clockwise from there. Nobody starts with any cards in hand. You will draw cards at the start of your very first turn.

summary.title: Gather All the Cards
bullets: 162 total cards: 144 numbered (1-12) + 18 Skip-Bo wilds
Combine into one deck

summary.title: Shuffle the Deck
bullets: Shuffle all 162 cards thoroughly
Mix wilds and numbers evenly

Do not look at the cards

summary.title: Place the Draw Pile
bullets: Remaining cards go to center as draw pile

summary.title: Pick Who Goes First
bullets: Choose starting player, play clockwise
No cards in hand to start — draw on first turn

walkthrough.title: The Goal of Skip-Bo
walkthrough.text: The goal is simple. Be the first player to get rid of every card in your stock pile. That is it. You do this by playing cards onto shared building piles in the center. The building piles count up from one to twelve, and when a pile hits twelve, it gets cleared away.

walkthrough.title: Draw Up to Five
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, draw cards from the draw pile until you have five cards in your hand. On your very first turn, you will draw all five since your hand starts empty. On later turns, you might only need to draw one or two to refill back up to five.

walkthrough.title: Play Cards to Building Piles
walkthrough.text: Now you can start playing cards to the center. Building piles must start with a one and go up in order, all the way to twelve. You can play cards from three sources: your hand, the top card of your stock pile, or the top card of any of your discard piles. You can play as many cards as you want on your turn.

walkthrough.title: How Building Piles Work
walkthrough.text: There can be up to four building piles in the center at once. Each one starts with a one and builds sequentially. So after one comes two, then three, and so on up to twelve. When a building pile reaches twelve, it is complete. Scoop it up, shuffle those cards back into the draw pile, and that space is open for a new pile.

walkthrough.title: Skip-Bo Wild Cards
walkthrough.text: The Skip-Bo cards are wildcards. They can stand in for any number from one to twelve. Need a seven to keep a building pile going? Play a Skip-Bo card as a seven. They are incredibly powerful, so use them wisely. Try to save them for moments when they help you play from your stock pile.

walkthrough.title: End Your Turn
walkthrough.text: When you cannot or do not want to play any more cards, you must discard one card from your hand onto one of your four personal discard piles. This ends your turn. You always end by discarding, even if you could keep playing. Be strategic about which discard pile you place it on.

walkthrough.title: Your Discard Piles
walkthrough.text: You have four personal discard piles. Only the top card of each pile is available to play on future turns. Organize them wisely. A good trick is to keep numbers in descending order so smaller numbers stay accessible on top. You can look through your discard piles at any time to plan ahead.

walkthrough.title: Playing All Five Cards
walkthrough.text: Here is a great bonus rule. If you manage to play all five cards from your hand in a single turn, immediately draw five more cards and keep going. You do not have to discard. This can lead to exciting chain turns where you play ten or even fifteen cards in one go.

summary.title: The Goal of Skip-Bo
bullets: First player to empty their stock pile wins
Play cards onto shared building piles (1→12)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: We will walk through a practice round with three players. Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3 each have their stock pile with the top card flipped up. The center is empty with no building piles yet. Let's deal some hands and start playing.

walkthrough.title: Check Your Stock Piles
walkthrough.text: Let's see what everyone is working with. Player 1 has a three showing on top of their stock pile. Player 2 has a seven. Player 3 has a one. Remember, the goal is to empty your stock pile, so every time you play that top card, you flip the next one and keep going.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Draws and Plays
walkthrough.text: Player 1, draw five cards from the draw pile. Let's say you drew a one, a four, a six, a nine, and a Skip-Bo. Great news — you have a one! Play it to the center to start the first building pile. Now play your Skip-Bo card as a two. Then play your stock pile's three on top of that. Flip your next stock pile card. Then play the four from your hand. Nice chain!

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Ends Their Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 1 still has a six and a nine in hand. Neither one can go on the building pile, which needs a five next. Time to discard. Let's put the nine on discard pile one and keep the six accessible. Wait, actually, let's put the six on a separate discard pile so it stays on top. Discard the nine to end your turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Draws and Plays
walkthrough.text: Player 2, draw five cards. You have a five, a two, an eight, a ten, and a three. The building pile needs a five — play it! Now the pile needs a six. You do not have a six, but check your options. Nothing works, so it is time to discard. Put the eight on one discard pile and end your turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring the Stock Pile
walkthrough.text: Player 3 draws five cards and gets a one, a two, a five, an eleven, and a Skip-Bo. The building pile needs a six, and Player 3 does not have one. But wait — Player 3's stock pile shows a one. Player 3 plays the one and two from hand to start a new building pile, then plays the Skip-Bo as a three. But they completely forgot to check their stock pile. Their stock pile's one could have started that second building pile instead, saving the Skip-Bo for later.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Prioritize Your Stock Pile
walkthrough.text: The number one rule in Skip-Bo is to play from your stock pile whenever possible. Your stock pile card should always be your first choice. Player 3 wasted a valuable Skip-Bo card when their stock pile had the exact number they needed. Always check your stock pile top card before playing from your hand.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws back up to five cards. They check their stock pile first — good habit. The stock pile shows a ten. The building piles need a six and a four. Player 1 cannot play from the stock pile right now, so they play from hand and discard piles to advance the building piles. They discard to end their turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Helping the Next Player
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws up to five. They have a six and can play it on the first building pile. But look at the situation. Player 3's stock pile now shows a seven. If Player 2 plays the six, the pile will need a seven next, and Player 3 will play it right off their stock pile. Player 2 plays the six anyway without thinking about it. Player 3 immediately benefits on their next turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what numbers your opponents have showing on their stock piles. If playing a card would set up the next player to empty their stock pile card, consider holding that card and discarding something else instead. Sometimes the best play is the card you do not play.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Messy Discard Piles
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has been tossing cards onto discard piles randomly. They put a three on top of a two, and a nine on top of a five. Now when they need that two or five later, those cards are buried and unplayable. Meanwhile, Player 1 has been neatly organizing their discards with higher numbers on top of lower ones.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Organize Your Discard Piles
walkthrough.text: Treat your four discard piles like organized storage, not a junk drawer. Keep them sorted so useful cards stay on top. A common strategy is to put higher numbers on top so lower numbers stay accessible. Use all four piles and try to keep cards in sequences when you can.

walkthrough.title: A Big Chain Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws up to five and gets lucky. They play all five cards in a row onto the building piles. Since they played their entire hand, they draw five more cards and keep going. They manage to play three more before running out of moves. That is eight cards in one turn! They discard the last two to end.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Great job! You have played through the core of Skip-Bo. You have seen how to draw, play to building piles, use wild cards, and manage your discard piles. Remember the three big lessons: always prioritize your stock pile, watch what your opponents need, and keep your discard piles organized. You are ready to play a full game on your own. Before you start, check out General Tips for more strategy advice, and use the Appendix for quick reference. Ask your personal GM Guide anytime you have a question during play. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Stock Pile First, Always
walkthrough.text: The single most important habit in Skip-Bo is to play from your stock pile whenever you can. Every card you play from the stock pile brings you one step closer to winning. Before you play anything from your hand, check whether your stock pile's top card fits on any building pile. If it does, play it first.

walkthrough.title: Save Your Skip-Bo Cards
walkthrough.text: Skip-Bo wild cards are your most powerful resource. Do not waste them just to keep a building pile moving. Save them for moments when playing a wild lets you get a stock pile card out. The ideal use of a Skip-Bo card is to bridge a gap that lets you play your stock pile's top card immediately after.

walkthrough.title: Organize Your Discard Piles
walkthrough.text: Your four discard piles are like a secondary hand. Keep them organized so you can access useful cards later. A reliable strategy is to place higher numbers on top and lower numbers underneath. Use all four piles and try to group numbers that are close together. A well-organized discard area can save you many turns.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Always look at what number is showing on each opponent's stock pile. If playing a card from your hand would set up the next player to dump their stock pile card, think twice. Sometimes holding a card and discarding something else is the smarter move. Denying opponents their key numbers is just as important as advancing your own stock pile.

walkthrough.title: Aim for Five-Card Chains
walkthrough.text: If you play all five cards from your hand in one turn, you draw five more and keep going. These chain turns can be game-changing. When you see an opportunity to play four cards, look hard for a way to play that fifth. Even if the fifth card does not help your stock pile directly, the five-card redraw is often worth it.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Rush Building Piles
walkthrough.text: Just because you can play a card on a building pile does not mean you should. Consider who benefits. If advancing a pile helps an opponent more than it helps you, hold back. Patience is a real strategy in Skip-Bo. Focus on moves that directly get your stock pile cards played or set up your discard piles for future turns.

walkthrough.title: Team Variant Tips
walkthrough.text: If you are playing the team variant with four or six players, remember that you can play cards from your partner's stock pile on your turn. Communicate openly about what numbers you each need. Prioritize getting your partner's stock pile cards out when you can, because either partner emptying their stock pile wins the game for your team.

summary.title: Team Variant Tips
bullets: Can play from partner's stock pile on your turn
Communicate about needed numbers
Either partner emptying stock pile wins for the team

walkthrough.title: Discard Pile Sequencing
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat their four discard piles as a secondary hand of playable cards. The key technique is to build descending sequences within each pile. For example, put a nine on a pile, then an eight on top, then a seven. When the building pile reaches seven, you can chain three cards off one discard pile. This takes discipline but pays off massively.

walkthrough.title: Counting Key Cards
walkthrough.text: There are twelve copies of each number in the deck. If you have seen eight sevens go by in building piles, you know only four remain in the entire game. Tracking which numbers are scarce helps you decide when to hold a card versus when to play it. Pay special attention to numbers near your stock pile's top card.

walkthrough.title: Blocking Strategy
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best play is no play at all. If your opponent's stock pile shows a five and the building pile is sitting at three, do not play a four from your hand if you can avoid it. Force your opponent to waste their own cards or Skip-Bo wilds to bridge that gap. Blocking becomes more important as the game nears its end.

walkthrough.title: Skip-Bo Card Timing
walkthrough.text: The optimal use of a Skip-Bo card is to create a chain that empties two or more stock pile cards in one turn. For example, if your stock pile shows a six and the building pile is at four, play a Skip-Bo as five, then play your six from the stock pile. If your next stock pile card happens to be seven, you keep going. Save wilds for these explosive turns.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Draw Pile
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, completed building piles get shuffled back into the draw pile. This means the draw pile's composition changes over time. If many low numbers were just cleared from building piles, the draw pile is now richer in low numbers. Use this knowledge to predict what you might draw on future turns.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Acceleration
walkthrough.text: When your stock pile gets down to the last five or six cards, shift into aggressive mode. Start using Skip-Bo cards more freely. Play every card you can from your stock pile, even if it helps opponents slightly. At this stage, tempo matters more than blocking. One great chain turn can end the game before anyone else catches up.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Game Scoring Strategy
walkthrough.text: In the multi-game scoring variant, the winner gets 25 points plus five points per card remaining in each opponent's stock pile. This means blowout wins are worth much more. If you are far ahead, try to win quickly before opponents reduce their stock piles. If you are behind, focus on reducing your stock pile even if you cannot win this round, to minimize the points you give away.

summary.title: Discard Pile Sequencing
bullets: Build descending sequences in each discard pile
Example: 9→8→7 on one pile → chain 3 cards later
Discipline now = big payoff later

summary.title: Counting Key Cards
bullets: 12 copies of each number in the deck
Track which numbers are scarce
Focus on numbers near your stock pile's needs

summary.title: Blocking Strategy
bullets: Don't play cards that set up opponent's stock pile
Force opponents to use their own wilds
More important as game nears end

summary.title: Skip-Bo Card Timing
bullets: Best use: bridge a gap → chain 2+ stock pile cards
Example: wild as 5 → stock pile 6 → stock pile 7
Save wilds for explosive stock pile chain turns

summary.title: Reading the Draw Pile
bullets: Completed building piles reshuffle into draw pile

Predict draws based on recently cleared piles

Ahead? Win fast before opponents shrink stock piles
Behind? Minimize stock pile to reduce points given away

entries.definition: Your personal pile of 30 (or 20) face-down cards with the top card visible. Emptying this pile is how you win the game.
The shared central deck that players draw from at the start of each turn. Replenished by completed building piles.
Up to four shared piles in the center that build sequentially from one to twelve. When a pile reaches twelve, it is shuffled back into the draw pile.
Each player has four personal discard piles. Only the top card of each pile is playable. Organize strategically for future turns.
A wild card that can substitute for any number from one to twelve. There are 18 in the deck. Best saved for stock pile chain plays.
Cards numbered one through twelve, with twelve copies of each number in the deck for 144 total numbered cards.
The first thing you do on your turn: draw cards from the draw pile until you hold five cards in hand.
The main part of your turn where you play cards from hand, stock pile, or discard piles onto building piles. Play as many as you want.
The mandatory end of your turn. Place one card from your hand onto one of your four personal discard piles.
If you play all five cards from your hand in one turn, draw five new cards immediately and continue playing without discarding.
A building pile that reaches twelve. It is removed from the center, shuffled, and added back to the draw pile. The space opens for a new pile.
With four or six players, partners sit across from each other. You may play from your partner's stock pile on your turn. Team wins when either partner empties their stock pile.
Deal 20 cards per stock pile instead of 30 for a faster game. All other rules remain the same.
Optional scoring across multiple games. Winner scores 25 points plus five points per card remaining in each opponent's stock pile. Play to 500 points.
